_short_description: Reproduces the Ulrich (1984) hospital-window-view condition shown to shorten post-surgical recovery and reduce analgesic use vs a brick-wall view condition. Parametric proxy: window onto natural scene, biophilic indoor elements, daylight, restful colour palette.
_published_finding: Ulrich (1984): cholecystectomy patients with bedside windows facing trees had shorter postoperative stays (M = 7.96 days vs 8.70), required fewer strong analgesics, and received fewer negative evaluative comments from nurses than matched patients with windows facing a brick wall. N = 46 matched pairs. The effect has been partially replicated in Park & Mattson (2009) for appendectomy and other studies (see Stress Recovery Theory; Ulrich et al., 1991).
_caveats: Ulrich's manipulation was a window VIEW, not the room contents. The parametric reproduction necessarily synthesises the view as a back-projected image; a researcher reproducing the experimental protocol should use real-photo backdrops rather than synthetic ones to avoid valence-of-rendering confounds.

Stress Recovery Theory (Ulrich et al., 1991) and Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan & Kaplan, 1989) overlap on this preset. The L17 (restorativeness) ART preset and this Ulrich SRT preset should produce similar parametric configurations but cite different theoretical mechanisms.

Cross-cultural variance for clinical-environment response is documented in Beauchemin & Hays (1998) replication and Park & Mattson (2008) on Mediterranean hospitals. Stay alert to non-Western hospital aesthetics where 'clinical' colour palettes differ.

The preset's effect is on RECOVERY MEASURES (length of stay, analgesic use), not directly on a Sprint-2 latent. Closest latents: cognitive.restorativeness (L17), cognitive.care_signal (L33 — clinical vs warm), cognitive.coziness (L25).

_citation.apa_primary: Ulrich, R. S. (1984). View through a window may influence recovery from surgery. Science, 224(4647), 420–421.
apa_supporting: Ulrich, R. S., Simons, R. F., Losito, B. D., Fiorito, E., Miles, M. A., & Zelson, M. (1991). Stress recovery during exposure to natural and urban environments. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 11(3), 201–230.
Park, S.-H., & Mattson, R. H. (2009). Therapeutic influences of plants in hospital rooms on surgical recovery. HortScience, 44(1), 102–105.
